data.title: Guggenheim's GOF Defies Closed-End Fund Logic, For A Reason (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities Fund (NYSE:GOF) is a closed-end fixed-income fund with a liberal mandate. It takes a go-anywhere approach to fixed income and it's trading at a 30.21% premium to net asset value. It utilizes leverage, its discount/premium to NAV and its great investment team to generate highly impressive returns. It has managed to do so over an extended period of time.

I rarely, if ever, buy CEFs at a premium to net asset value. I’ve generally published bearish reports on GOF over the years. Alternating between hold and sell. The oldest one I could find dates back to November 2020. There is a reason for this "policy".

If I buy at a discount, I'd like the sentiment around the CEF or its asset class to improve. When sentiment is terrible this tends to happen over time. Even if it improves to indifference that's fine. If I buy at a premium there is usually a lot of hype or optimism about the underlying or the manager. This doesn't tend to last very long.

Meanwhile the returns on GOF have been great:

GOF is one of the rare funds that appears to be able to retain its premium. This time I want to review one important factor that may (in part) explain why this happens. The fund has a built-in way to issue shares which is through its DRIP program. The DRIP program allows investors to receive the dividend in the shape of newly issued shares. It is explained in GOF’s annual report:

When shares trade at a discount to NAV, the DRIP takes advantage of the discount by reinvesting the monthly dividend distribution in common shares of the Fund purchased in the market at a price less than NAV. Conversely, when the market price of the Fund’s common shares is at a premium above NAV, the DRIP reinvests participants’ dividends in newly issued common shares at the greater of NAV per share or 95% of the market price per share.

If you have the DRIP turned on you are currently acquiring shares at only ~123% of net asset value. Reflecting 95% of the market price. Acquiring shares at a discount to the market price is obviously preferable to buying them in the open market. However, you’re also consistently investing at above market prices.

Personally, I’d turn this feature off at this large premium to net asset value.

It is advantageous if you expect the premium to last or rise. Something that’s been the case recently. The feature is also one reason you could argue in favor of remaining invested when you don't love the valuation. Especially if you expect other investors to continue investing at higher premiums than you’ve paid. Otherwise, it seems unwise to keep participating and buy shares at an ever greater premium while new shares are being issued continuously.

It seems great to buy a fund like this once it trades at a very attractive valuation (in terms of discount), turn off the drip once the premium to net asset value exceeds ~6% and then enjoy the additional value creation of shares being issued to other shareholders at large premia.

This drip feature coupled with the fund’s long and impressive track record does argue for holding on to the fund at a premium to net asset value that would otherwise make me a seller. Even when the fund is far from a buy (or DRIP) it can be considered a hold.

At a 30% premium, with a distribution ratio of 15% (roughly the current level) the fund generates a return on net assets of around ~3% just through issuing shares. That’s provided all shareholders would drip (which they don’t as not all brokers offer it).

The DRIP appears to take value from all shareholders and benefit the DRIP shareholders. However, to get this “benefit” of overpaying less, the DRIP shareholder is consistently buying assets at a massive premium to NAV.

Ultimately, the distribution seems unsustainable to me. It forces the fund to distribute more and more cash or shares that are unlikely to be generated through returns on the underlying.

If we don’t consider the tailwinds/headwinds of the drip program for a second.

The fund is trading at 130% of net asset value with a ~15% distribution yield. The management team has to generate a 19.5% return on assets under management.

That seems incredibly challenging with a (mostly) fixed income portfolio with a yield-to-maturity of 7.83% and a leverage ratio of 15.63%.

At a higher premium, the question is less and less about the attractiveness of the underlying and the outlook for fixed income, or whether the management team can deliver enough alpha to warrant the premium (highly unlikely) and it becomes increasingly important how much the DRIP program benefits you. Of the latest distribution of ~$0.18 per share, 68% was classified as a return of capital.

GOF has never traded above 38% of its net asset value. It has traded at a significant discount to NAV at times. The average premium is around 9%. A very high and impressive premium given most funds tend to trade at a discount and the average “premium” is negative.

Personally, I’d at least turn off the drip at these valuations. The risk of turning the drip program off is that NAV increases further or remains steady while other shareholders dilute you by enjoying the discount. Here are three examples of future scenarios:

Future Premium/Discount

Likely better total return

Reverts to average premium of 9%

Cash dividend

Premium stays 30%+

DRIP

Distribution Cut Premium Crash

Cash dividend

I acknowledge that there is a path where having the DRIP turned on is quite beneficial. However, it seems like a narrow path. Keep in mind that once the premium starts converging towards NAV the calculations change. Once we’re near ~106% of NAV I’ll be much less opposed to having the DRIP turned on.

Meanwhile, the average closed-end fund trades at a 4% discount. The average fixed-income fund trades at a 2.66% discount. This fund is clearly much better than average. There is a strong team behind it. It has a wide-ranging mandate and it uses variable amounts of leverage (indicating there is consideration to it). Its historical performance has been excellent. I think this is either a hold or a sell. In the end, I'll put a hold rating on it. The Fed is signalling interest rate cuts. The premium to NAV is still going higher. I don't see why the premium would collapse in the near term. Keep in mind that it could happen. The large premium gives a lot of runway to a potential drawdown.

data.title: CPZ: Discount Narrowing Drives Strong Results, But Now Takes Away Its Appeal
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Calamos L/S Equity & Dynamic Income Trust (CPZ) provides investors with exposure to a portfolio split between equity and fixed-income investments. In the fixed-income sleeve, it is primarily allocated to high-yield corporate debt, but can also include investment-grade corporate debt, syndicated loans, securitized debt, and preferred shares.

Setting this fund apart from other hybrid funds is that they also utilize a short-selling strategy within the fund. That's actually similar to the Clough Global Opportunities Fund (GLO), which we covered very recently.

Since our last update, the fund has performed incredibly well—which is great to see, even if it is a fund that I don't find particularly attractive. This is especially true as the fund's discount has narrowed quite materially in the last year. I maintain a "hold" rating, taking a more neutral stance on this fund.

CPZ Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 2.97
  • Discount/Premium: -4.71%
  • Distribution Yield: 10.01%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.92%
  • Leverage: 25.62%
  • Managed Assets: $468.3 million
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation date, November 25, 2031)

CPZ's investment objective is to "seek to provide current income and risk-managed capital appreciation." They also add that the fund "seeks to provide hedged market exposure built around Calamos' time-tested global long/short equity strategy." The portfolio fit they mention is, "the fund may be appropriate for risk-conscious investors who want to put capital to work in pursuit of income and capital appreciation."

The fund is leveraged, and that can increase overall risk due to amplified downside moves, though it does also allow for outsized upside moves when things are going well, too. When including the borrowing costs, the fund's total expense ratio comes in at 3.23%. The fund's expense ratio is on the higher side, even when excluding the leverage costs. This is because when they are selling short, there is an added expense of dividend and interest expense on those short sales.

Performance: Discount Narrowing

The fund has seen strong performance since our last update, beating out the S&P 500 Index—which is not a direct benchmark, but can provide overall context. With CPZ outperforming, that can be quite a feat during this time, considering it also includes short positions and fixed-income holdings.

With that said, while that is quite impressive, a lot of the results here were from the discount narrowing quite materially during this period. We can see exactly how much that has helped to boost the fund's total share price returns during this time when we split out the total NAV returns and compare that to its total share price return and SPY during the same time.

Of course, this is one of the benefits that comes with closed-end funds: the opportunity to potentially realize these sorts of large discounts. Ideally, we primarily look at relative historical discounts rather than the absolute discount of a fund alone. At some point, such a deep discount can make it an interesting choice if there is potential for activist pressure to push the fund to take actions to realize that discount.

Currently, CPZ is owned by well-known activist group Saba Capital Management and Sit Investments Associates, both owning about 5% currently. Saba has held a position for years now, and the latest move was trimming some of their holdings in late 2024. With the discount narrowing now to mid-single digits, it becomes less of a tempting opportunity on that front. If the fund dropped to a double-digit level again, though, it would certainly make it more interesting.

Distribution: Steady Monthly Payout

Since the fund's inception in late 2019, they have bumped up their payout a few times initially. The final time was in mid-2021, when the regular monthly distribution has been since. More recently, they paid out a rather large year-end special last year. The payout currently works out to a distribution rate of 10.01%, with a NAV distribution rate of 9.53%.

Since the fund's inception, they've paid a total cumulative $9.368.

To cover the distribution, CPZ will rely primarily on being able to realize gains from its underlying portfolio. That isn't all that uncommon, particularly for a fund that is tilted toward a heavy equity sleeve combined with a short sale strategy.

Net investment income coverage in the latest reported semi-annual posting came in at 16.5%. That was down from the last fiscal year, which was closer to 28%.

For tax purposes, the fund classified its 2024 distributions as a combination of qualified dividends, ordinary income, and short-term capital gains. The largest of these was short-term capital gains, which can make it less tax-friendly and more appropriate for a tax-sheltered account.

A small portion this year has also been estimated to be return of capital, which is generally tax-friendly as a way to defer tax obligations. Still, the largest characterization is expected to be STCG.

CPZ's Portfolio

More broadly, CPZ's exposure is primarily tilted toward its long/short component, at 70.7% of the portfolio. The preferred component stands at 13.7%, and the fixed-income sleeve is 15.6%. Within the long/short, the fund is tilted toward more long positions.

The largest positions on the long side include several of the Magnificent 7 names, with Amazon (AMZN) as the largest, NVIDIA (NVDA) coming in as the second largest, and then Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) also making an appearance.

Interestingly, when looking at the short side of the portfolio, they are primarily shorting the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

This is part of my main hesitation with finding CPZ appealing. The largest holding in SPY is NVDA these days; AMZN and GOOGL are also included, and those three positions account for 14.1% of SPY currently. Meaning that while they are long these positions, they are then shorting them indirectly as well. They are also showing a short Apple (AAPL) position, which is another 6.33% of SPY, meaning they are essentially short to a larger degree than the 1.1% listed above.

With that said, when the market pulls back or corrects, as we saw in April of this year, this fund acts as a hedge to the downside. We saw that play out in the above total NAV return chart as well. SPY declined meaningfully, and CPZ outperformed during that time. On the flip side, the total NAV returns gave up a lot of the recovery—as we would expect to see.

Conclusion

CPZ provides a hedge against the overall market given its short exposure. Shorting takes a bit of timing to do successfully, but CPZ is consistently short regularly. Meaning they aren't necessarily looking to manage their short position actively, but provide regular short exposure on a regular basis, primarily via SPY. At least that has been the case from what we've seen now covering this fund over the years. I believe that's what makes it a relatively less appealing fund to consider unless it trades at an outsized discount, which is not currently the case.

data.title: Sell Super Micro Computer Stock At Your Own Risk (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI), aka Supermicro stock, is flying in pre-market trading on Friday, up around 6%.

Supermicro has begun large-scale shipments of Nvidia’s (NVDA) Blackwell Ultra systems and rack-level plug-and-play data center solutions, announcing on September 11, 2025, that it is now delivering Nvidia HGX B300 systems and GB300 NVL72 at volume to customers worldwide. By offering fully pre-validated solutions across systems, racks, and complete data center environments through its Data Center Building Block Solutions, the company enables day-one deployment with fewer integration delays. The company rolled out new AI systems built on Nvidia’s Blackwell back in June to speed up AI factory setups in Europe, with over 10 models now available.

With a portfolio of more than 10 SKUs, featuring both Nvidia Blackwell and Blackwell Ultra platforms, Supermicro provides customers with the flexibility to build diverse AI factory environments at virtually any scale, positioning itself as a top supplier in the market. The only question that remains is, can Supermicro sustain revenue growth?

According to the company's president and CEO, Charles Liang:

Supermicro has the best track record of fast and successful deployments of new Nvidia technologies…Through Supermicro Data Center Building Block Solutions with our expertise in on-site deployment, we enable turn-key delivery of the highest-performance AI platform — critical for customers seeking to invest in cutting-edge technology. Data center customers face many AI infrastructure challenges: complex network topology and cabling, power delivery, and thermal management. Supermicro delivers pre-validated, plug-and-play solutions at system, rack, and data center scale, enabling AI factories to deploy rapidly and helping our customers lead in AI.

The old and the new:

I last covered Supermicro following its fiscal Q4 earnings in early August, where weaker EPS and guidance sparked a 16% pullback despite continued strength in revenue growth and FY26 targets. Since then, shares have slipped another 6%. While margin pressures and high expectations remain challenges, I am upgrading the stock to a Strong Buy and continue to view the recent weakness as a buying opportunity given sustained AI-driven demand and attractive valuation multiples. Backed by its global manufacturing scale and strong hyperscaler relationships, the company is still positioned for more than 50% revenue growth in FY26 as AI data center demand accelerates.

While the stock is up around 44% year to date, against the S&P 500 (SP500), up 12.2%, I am continuing to recommend it at current levels.

The news further proves Supermicro’s ties with Nvidia, whose GPUs are the backbone of AI computing, despite intensified competition. I liked Supermicro at $35, and I liked it at $46, and I continue to like it at $43.

Risks

But I was wary of the stock back in mid-May.

I downgraded Super Micro to a Sell in mid-May after a 40% run, noting that the Saudi DataVolt deal had already been priced into the stock. Shares have traded roughly flat since. My concern was around unsold H100 GPU inventory and ongoing margin pressures, which made near-term financial improvement difficult. Furthermore, at the time, the company’s FY26 revenue target of $40 billion appeared overly ambitious and at risk of downward revision, with management still showing limited confidence and visibility at the time. Most of these stand, but my view has shifted as the risk/reward profile has improved.

With the stock consolidating after months of sideways action, I now see an attractive entry point. In my view, Supermicro is positioned to surprise to the upside as execution and visibility into FY26 improve, making the case for my renewed bullish stance. I think the pre-market action shows that investors are beginning to recognize the strength of Supermicro’s positioning as Nvidia’s closest AI hardware partner. The company’s ability to rapidly ship next-generation Blackwell Ultra systems at scale is a clear competitive advantage, and as AI infrastructure spending continues to accelerate, I expect Supermicro to capture a disproportionate share of that growth, as I discussed in detail in my last coverage.

The risk is there, but the stock’s muted performance since May has reset expectations, creating a favorable setup for a re-rating higher, in my opinion. To keep a long story short, sell Super Micro at your own risk.

Valuation:

Supermicro’s valuation remains cheap at current levels. If Supermicro sustains revenue growth ahead of the broader sector, which I believe it will, and simultaneously expands into higher-margin offerings like DCBBS amid the ongoing AI boom, the stock looks significantly undervalued. According to the company’s Senior Vice President of Corporate Development, Michael Staiger, at Citi's 2025 Global Technology, Media, and Telecommunications Conference last week:

With respect to sovereigns, there are licensing elements. It's a factor, like, have they had the licenses, can they ship? Have they spec-ed out the data center, right? So that kind of goes back to what we are seeing, what we're doing with DCBBS, where we can take — we can retrofit a data center for them, bring the whole experience together. We have the leading edge from a liquid-cooled element.

Looking at only the forward valuation metrics, Supermicro is trading at a forward P/E of 16.84, about 31% below the sector median, and a forward GAAP P/E of 19.70, nearly 38% lower. Its PEG Non-GAAP forward ratio sits at 0.65, a steep discount to the sector’s 1.82. This screams undervalued to me.

Price movement:

The stock is currently consolidating under the EMA200 at $44.6, and if it can reclaim and hold above $45 with volume, I think we could see a path towards +$50. Support is currently around $39-$40, and a breakdown below that risks revisiting the $34 level. RSI is recovering from oversold territory and is turning bullish, currently at 48. Recent volume spikes align with selloffs, and the bounce has come on lighter volume so far. Ideally, I want to see stronger volume to confirm a reversal. This is a make-or-break spot, and I’m betting on the former.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Its Energy Empire Is Better Than The Rock Of The Midstream
data.description:
Introduction

I have been following the Midstream industry since 2021, when most valuations in the sphere became depressed due to the ongoing COVID-19 crisis. I believe that the industry is one of the most fascinating and interesting for income-focused investors. The stability of the businesses means that they’re perfect for distribution and they are less reliant on the underlying commodity prices compared to upstream or downstream companies.

I’ve covered many companies in the field, and I now want to do a heads-up comparison with what I believe could be the best two options in the midstream segment: Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET) and Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD). The two offer different value propositions, and I believe that they will be great for different sets of investors.

A quick reminder

I will review two MLPs in this analysis, which are fundamentally different from regular C-corporations like Apple Inc. (AAPL) or Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM). They have different tax treatments, as you’re not technically a shareholder when investing in an MLP but a limited partner that holds units in the structure. These partnerships don’t technically pay corporate tax, but instead, the profits and losses are passed through to the unitholders.

Unitholders have to file a K-1 form that is more complex than a simple 1099-DIV and breaks down your share of the partnership, credits and deductions. Though the distributions are considered Return of Capital, which is not directly taxed as income. It lowers your cost basis and allows you to defer taxes until you sell your units. There are, therefore, some considerations to invest in the MLP space as it adds tax complexity to a unitholders portfolio.

Energy Transfer: The Energy Empire

I’ve covered Energy Transfer a couple of times and it has been one of the longest holdings of mine and has rewarded me greatly over the years with both unit price appreciation as well as great distribution. It is one of the largest energy companies in the country and has exposure to absolutely all types of fossil fuel, from refined products to LNG to crude. It also includes strategic investments in Sunoco and USA Compression Partners, diversifying its presence in the energy value chain.

More recently, ET has increased its CapEx targets as it intends to invest over $5B in organic growth projects in 2025, which is significantly up from the past couple of years, as the company expects increased US electricity demand, mainly in Texas, where ET has focused on connecting its assets to various power plants. ET is also facing strong demand from Data centers that want to bypass utilities and directly contract energy supply.

Energy Transfer has a vast set of assets with an extensive pipeline network that reaches around 140,000 miles connecting all major US production basins to key demand centers as well as export hubs on the Gulf Coast.

Regarding natural gas, the company has an intricate system of both interstate and intrastate pipelines that includes the Transwestern, which is currently undergoing an increase in capacity from the Permian. The Rover Pipeline is also critical to transporting gas from the Marcellus and Utica shale deposits. The Intrastate network is also a key component of its portfolio, and I believe that the company’s interstate segment offers a great upside to Energy Transfer, as the segment offers huge barriers to entry and massive scale advantages.

For crude oil, some key assets include the well-known Dakota Access Pipeline as well as ETCOP, which connects the Bakken Shale to various storage hubs and refineries. The Permian Express has also become key to ET’s operation, tapping into the prolific Permian Basin and shipping crude to the Gulf Coast markets. NGLs and refined products include the Mariner East Pipeline system as well as the Lone Star NGL pipeline, which connect the Marcellus/Utica shales and the Permian Basin to Mont Belvieu, respectively.

ET also has a massive, strictly midstream operation that includes the Nederland Terminal, which is a world-class facility on the Gulf Coast and handles both the import and export of ET’s petroleum products. Other assets in the category include Mont Belvieu and various crude storage facilities across the country.

If we dig into what makes Energy Transfer really special, the energy empire has an unmatched scale that is now highly diversified, which can provide a certain hedge to a singular commodity weakness. Regarding risk, ET has been strengthening its balance sheet in order to achieve an investment-grade credit rating. And this combines with a more disciplined capital allocation that has been the previous problem for ET, as it used to do a lot of deals to expand its operation, which has been a tad aggressive in the past. With the focus on organic growth, I believe that ET could be even more rewarding towards unitholders. Furthermore, I believe that ET’s management is world-class, as it has been able to build an unparalleled infrastructure footprint.

Enterprise Products Partners: The Rock of the Midstream

EPD is also a titan in the midstream energy industry as it is a leading provider of natural gas, NGLs, crude, and other petroleum-derived products. It is well-renowned for its financial discipline and conservative management, which has helped the company navigate even the most tumultuous times of the industry. And compared to ET, EPD is more focused on being stable and generating constant DCF instead of focusing on controlling the whole energy midstream segment. The fee-based model of the company allows it to protect its earnings from direct commodity price volatility.

EPD’s management recently discussed its capital priorities, and with its mix of contracted revenue, we should see even more return to unitholders as EPD should limit its growth capital in the near future. Figures are suggesting a potential 71% of DCF being redistributed to unitholders. Though the company is focusing mostly on its NGL segment, with Permian gas and various NGL processing facilities. The firm is also expanding its export infrastructure in the Gulf as US NGL exports have been running at full speed over the past couple of years. This will also be coupled with rising NGL production as wells tend to produce more gas and NGLs as they get older.

The NGL P/S operation is certainly EPD’s biggest value proposition, as it captures most of the supply chain, offering great advantages for customers. Furthermore, NGLs tend to generate more fees than regular crude gathering because they are under more advantageous terms. I believe that this segment has an enormous moat, as EPD has direct access to international markets with its marine terminals. This allows Enterprise to either sell in the US or on international markets, depending on prices, allowing the company to directly pick the best potential margins.

The company’s crude segment is also highly robust, possessing some of the most crucial pipelines in the country, tapping into both the Permian and Cushing. Some key assets include the Midland-to-ECHO pipeline system as well as the Seaway Pipeline system, which are seeing high utilization rates due to their crucial positioning. EPD also has a natural gas segment that consists of intrastate networks in Louisiana and Texas. The contracts are mostly fixed-fee with no take-or-pay provisions that could reduce overall profitability for the segment.

Now I believe that the company will continue to maintain its financial fortress thanks to its peer-leading credit rating while expanding capacity on current routes, with measures such as the temporary conversion of the Midland-to-ECHO 2 while the company builds the Bahia pipeline. It is important to note that generally EPD focuses on funding growth via its own retained DCF instead of the excess use of debt, which would leave it more vulnerable in a down cycle.

I also believe that EPD has an unmatched NGL dominance with its massive integrated network that has a “wellhead-to-water” control over the entire value chain, creating great economies of scale and a highly competitive moat hard to replicate by other competitors. The company's balance sheet is set as a fortress, capable of weathering any financial storm, akin to the approach of JPMorgan in the banking industry, in my opinion. I think that’s why so many income-focused investors like the company, especially with its long history of consecutive annual distribution increases.

Valuation

Both ET and EPD have relatively in line valuation metrics compared to the broad industry, with an EV/EBITDA of 9.15 and 10.7, respectively, while ET has the advantage in terms of FCF yield at 9.4%, while EPD has a current 6.1% yield. EPD tends to generally command higher multiples as the business is deemed safer than Energy Transfer’s, as investors tend to put a premium on predictability. ET is broadly more complex and tends to have a higher leverage. Though I believe that ET has drastically changed its way of doing business and shunned aggressive acquisitions, paying down debt.

Regarding growth, the two companies differ with ET seeking transformative projects such as the recently approved Lake Charles LNG export facility, which could become another highly valuable income stream for the company and become a multi-billion dollar project that could reshape ET. On the other hand, EPD has the stable approach of incremental, low-risk investments, such as bolt-ons, like the acquisitions of certain assets from Western Midstream back in late 2024. EPD is also banking on its Petrochemicals segment, with expansion towards its downstream activity with projects such as the PDH plants that will add higher-value products such as Propylene. ET has been able to grow its EBITDA at a higher rate than EPD while its revenue has posted negative CAGR growth over the past 3 years due to ET’s revenue mix that has a larger component of its operation related to commodity-based contracts in which the company purchases and resells underlying energy commodities. Thus, the swing in prices has had a greater effect on ET compared to EPD. Regarding EBITDA, ET’s acquisitions, such as Crestwood and Enable, were directly accretive to the company’s profitability.

Both ET and EPD have similar margins despite having different approaches, as it resembles more of a Quality vs. Quantity dynamic. EPD is really a value chain integrator, as highlighted by its deep integration of its NGL segment and has been able to move higher-margin products. Regarding ET, it could be compared to the highway of fossil fuels with segments in each possible midstream operation. Though despite higher volumes, the transportation of natural and crude oil is essentially a “toll road” business that is generally lower-margin. Regarding return metrics, ET has slightly lower ones as it pursues more deals than EPD, thus depreciating metrics such as ROIC.

For distribution, ET currently has a higher yield at 7.5% compared to EPD’s 6.8% though EPD has a stronger track record with an unbroken 25+ year streak of consecutive increases. EPD is highly predictable in terms of the reliability of distribution that investors expect throughout any economic cycles. While ET famously cut its distribution to unitholders back in 2020 to redirect cash flow towards strengthening the balance sheet during the tumultuous times of the COVID period, which certainly offered a great opportunity to buy units. ET has since drastically increased its distribution to a higher level than pre-2020, as it achieved its leverage targets.

EPD has a better balance sheet and a better grasp on debt levels as it posts better overall metrics. As mentioned earlier, EPD has a great credit rating at A- from S&P, which allows Enterprise to have lower interest payments, and it targets a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3.5x. ET has been improving its credit rating over the past 4 years to now reach BBB, and the midstream company also has a leverage target range of 4x-4.5x debt-to-EBITDA, which the company has been successful in reaching.

Conclusion

With this full analysis behind us, I’ll start by saying that both of these companies are great; in fact, I rated them both as Strong Buys in previous analyses, and I still rate them as such. But the hard part comes now: which one of Energy Transfer or Enterprise Products Partners is the best company to invest in? I believe that EPD is a better value for retirees who rely on conservative and safe income, while ET offers greater total return potential and goes more towards value investor profiles. In general, I give the advantage to Energy Transfer, as I believe that it could offer the best growth prospects heading forward.

Risks

The two of them are large infrastructure-based companies that are operating in an industry that is currently being challenged by renewables. If the transition were to accelerate, this would reduce throughput volumes and hurt overall revenue. Furthermore, the two midstream companies would incur significant write-offs of their large asset base as well.

The risk of commodity price swings is limited, as midstream companies are dependent on volumes and not necessarily prices, though if their customers were to go bankrupt, this would significantly hurt them. ET is slightly more exposed than EPD to this kind of risk.

data.title: Cipher Mining: Building For The Future, But Still Bleeding
data.description:
I have written about Cipher Mining (NASDAQ:CIFR) a while back, and it always looked like a leveraged play on BTC. Q2 2025 earnings just proved this – revenue from mining is growing, but losses are increasing even faster. This seems like the cost structure is becoming problematic and revenue is not catching up.

The balance seems to be strong – cash is growing, equity as well. This gives financial reserves runway to survive bad quarters. Still, profitability is a risk, until the company solves their losses, it's unclear how long this runway will last.

Q2 Financial Results

CIFR reported their Q2 results and still they show signs of strong balance and growing revenue, but at the same time losses are making it risky. Revenue from BTC mining was $43.6 million, compared to $36.8 million a year ago. This is 18% growth, which shows that operations are growing. Problem is that at the same time, the loss grew as well, almost 3 times, from -$15.3 million to -$45.8 million. Half year results show it even worse, in 2024 H1, the company had net income of $24.6 million, but in 2025 H1 it was -$84.8 million loss. Revenue in H1 was $92.5 million, compared to $84.9 million a year ago. To be fair, it signals to me that costs are increasing and revenue is not catching up.

Balance is good, cash reserves jumped to $62.7 million, compared to $5.6 million in 2024. This means that the company has reserves to continue operating even in bad quarters. Total assets increased to $1.02 billion (from $855 million). This seems to me like a strong foundation, which gives some security for investors.

Biggest problem in my opinion is lack of profitability. If Q2 loss was lower, I could even say that the quarter was ‘investing into growth’. But -$45.8 million, per quarter, when revenue is $43.6 million, shows that the structure is not good. This also does not give much security as the cash reserves might run out as well.

Good News

Bitcoin reserve increased. Current worth is somewhere around $112.1 million, when it was $92.7 a year back. This means that CIFR has bigger reserve for the future, which could be a financial amortization when markets fluctuate.

They had ATM emission as well, where they raised around $85.7 million and took a loan of $172.5 million by issuing convertible bonds with 1.75% coupon until 2030. This is quite cheap financing if you ask me, which gives them some runway money.

Infrastructure got investments too. Company invested $164.4 million into new equipment and $55.9 into Black Pearl. This means that they are increasing hash rate potential and strengthening position before next cycles.

Black Pearl

One of the biggest catalysts I believe happened in Q2 2025 is new data center construction in Texas. The project is called Black Pearl, and it should generate 300 MW. Good news its already connected to the grid.

First phase has started with 150 MW power. The rest of it should be turned on in following quarters. Investments into the project were somewhat more than $126 million, most of it went to construction, infrastructure and equipment. CIFR expects that Black Pearl will increase hashrate and will become main revenue source.

This project showed that CIFR not only talks but at the same time builds. And that they can coordinate such projects. The difference from competitors is that they already have a working data center.

Only one thing I noticed, is that Black Pearl does not have fixed electricity pricing. So that means that costs can vary depending on the electricity market and spot prices. From one side, this means they can sell excess power and get some revenue, from other that means they can get some pressure on their revenue as well.

Risks

I have told about the losses, but lets dive deeper into them. The depreciation in Q2 2025 was $44 million, almost double compared to $20 million a year ago. And as I said, infrastructure is growing, that is good, but depreciation will increase with it as well, so we might see pressure on results.

Derivatives brought losses as well. Luminant Power Agreement value fell and cost -$15.5 million. This is a good reminder that financial instruments are risky to everyone and especially to the company which relies on them.

So, the worst thing is that the company’s equity is growing not through profits, but through emissions, which dilutes current shareholders and increases risk, while ROI stays low. At the same time, issuing bonds is just increasing burden.

Another thing is cash flow. Part of the revenue comes from selling BTC, which is considered investments. This means that operational results are weaker.

For portfolio building

I have taken a look at CIFR and BTC from econometric perspective. And the image is quite interesting. CIFR is like a leveraged play on BTC. When BTC goes up, CIFR goes higher even more, but it has the same effect when going down. When BTC goes down, so does CIFR, sometimes even more.

Correlation is another interesting part. If you look at the chart, from first sight it would look like they are correlating one to one, but in reality, correlation is around 0.27-0.36. This means that the connection exists but it’s not perfect. Why? Because daily numbers are noisy – CIFR has its own additional risks like operations, electricity pricing and so on. So this is why even though the direction of BTC and CIFR is the same, daily fluctuations may differ. I took weekly numbers and correlation immediately looks better, because the noise is fading. But, risk is daily, not weekly, this means that when retail investor spots that they ‘move together’, CIFR might have already made a move.

Granger causality and VAR models showed that BTC does not statistically predict CIFR. In other words, there is no model that shows that ‘BTC makes a move and CIFR follows’. Most of the time, they move together at the same time. VAR model showed instability, so that means that they both together make up really unstable system.

Volatility here shows the most interesting part. BTC volatility movements per day is somewhere between 3-5%, at the same time CIFR might be moving 20-40%. This does not mean that we might see the price increasing or decreasing in such numbers, this means that we might get days where this happens. So holding CIFR means you take more risk than holding BTC at this point.

What you should take from my rant? That CIFR is leveraged play on BTC, it may move and give you really big profits on bull markets, or give you a lot of losses on bear market. The charts and econometrics show the same, correlation exists, but causality doesn’t, but the risk is a few times higher. So, investing into CIFR means that you take the possibility of watching the stock moving irrational and volatile.

Summary

Cipher Mining Q2 2025 results show two sides. Revenue is increasing, the balance is strong, and cash reserves are growing. The company is investing into infrastructure, new projects like Black Pearl (which as mentioned already operational, and should become a catalyst). All this shows good direction.

But at the same time, the losses are risky. The company recorded quite large losses in H1 2025, when in H1 2024 they had profits. Rising depreciation and financial costs, profitability is questionable. Furthermore, part of the revenue is coming from BTC selling, not from operations, and share issuance dilutes current shareholders.

This is why I am ranking it hold, it's now just speculation on BTC, and I see too many risks, previously I was bullish, now it is time to take profits, and wait for new entries if they come.

data.title: Broadcom's Moat Is Now Impenetrable (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In my previous analysis, I laid out my reasons to start a position with Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO). Not that I want to do a victory dance or anything, but, since then, the stock has shot up over 47%.

Today, I still think it’s one of the most important companies in the future of the AI landscape. However, investors are more excited about NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) or Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) investments, while this $1.7 trillion semiconductor company is quietly positioning itself to capitalize on the next wave of AI surge.

With recent partnerships, financial performance, and growth catalysts in the pipelines, I’m upgrading my rating on Broadcom to a Strong Buy despite the emerging regulatory risk.

The $10 Billion AI Infrastructure

Starting with what I consider to be the most important development in recent history.

Broadcom has just secured a $10 billion order from a fourth hyperscaler for customized AI chips, which the company calls XPUs. Though the exact numbers weren’t explicitly stated, it's potentially between 1 and 2 million customized chips that will be placed in thousands of racks and nodes. This is also one of the largest customer AI processor deals in semiconductor history, and I think this will significantly affect how investors perceive Broadcom’s future.

This particular hyperscaler is believed to be OpenAI, who has always relied on Microsoft’s AI infrastructure throughout history. They spent billions on computing power to run ChatGPT. So, this $10 billion order indicates OpenAI’s shift towards proprietary hardware optimization, and this trend will very likely accelerate in the entire AI industry.

Why? Investing $10 billion in your AI infrastructure means that even just a 20% efficiency improvement would translate to $2 billion in savings. Not only that, hyperscalers often plan to reduce their reliance on a single source by turning to proprietary technologies.

This partnership also includes co-designing chips for inference workloads, which is where AI actually gets deployed and used by millions of people daily. Arguably, Microsoft has handled the training phase of ChatGPT, but OpenAI wants to use their own customized XPUs for the inference or the “doing” phase of AI.

Broadcom expects the orders to reflect in the “summer quarter” of 2025, while shipments are expected to commence in 2026. This also means OpenAI is securing its infrastructure for years ahead, and they’re betting on silicon rather than the off-the-shelf GPUs that Nvidia is selling. If it moves in the right direction, this would set Broadcom to capitalize on the next wave of AI infrastructure buildout as companies move to AI deployment at scale.

Custom silicon would also reduce costs compared to general-purpose GPUs, as companies can optimize their processors for specific needs. The question now is whether these XPUs will improve performance for particular cases, or will they be tagged as an alternative to Nvidia’s high-performance GPUs.

AI-Driven Revenue Momentum

On the actual company performance, Broadcom’s Q3 ‘25 AI revenue increased 63% to $5.2 billion, exceeding even the most bullish expectations. These figures also surpassed management's own expectations of $5.1 billion. This is also the 10th consecutive quarter of solid AI-driven growth, which is a solid indicator that this isn’t a simple flash in the pan but is driven by a real demand with notable momentum.

For Q4 ‘25, or the next quarter, management expects AI revenue to reach as much as $6.2 billion. If this materializes, it means the momentum is still strong and not decelerating like most have expected from such a high base. Management also stated that 2026 AI revenue growth will accelerate beyond the guided 50 to 60% range. I think this means Broadcom’s AI revenue could reach $35 to $45 billion by fiscal 2026. If that happens, AI would represent nearly half of total company revenue, which is a huge milestone in its transition to becoming an AI-first semiconductor company.

On the partnership front, Broadcom already services three confirmed hyperscalers before adding the fourth. These are Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and ByteDance (BDNCE) with their customer silicon. Each partnership represents a multi-year commitment, and when you design chips for these hyperscalers, you become part of the strategic planning for years to come. In this case, Broadcom is no longer as simple as a customized chip supplier, and switching costs would be significant.

As long as Alphabet continues to utilize TPUs, Meta with MTIAs, and ByteDance with their custom silicon, Broadcom will have a continuous stream of income. Customer concentration is definitely a risk factor, but the company has been adding new customers to its portfolio, so that risk factor appears to be dissipating over time.

That said, many are still skeptical about the likelihood of revenue growth in the following years, citing concerns that custom silicon won’t be as in demand as general-purpose GPUs, especially compared to the ones produced by Nvidia. Sure, I agree to some extent, but traditional data centers relied on these GPUs for all workloads, and they handled them reasonably well.

But AI workloads are different. Not more demanding than all aspects, but they need specialized accelerators that can handle massive parallel processing tasks, like multiplying matrices thousands of times per second. Broadcom, however, provides the complete intellectual property and network infrastructure that allows hyperscalers to build custom AI systems. The value creation is in the company’s pricing power and long-term contracts with hyperscalers worth billions of dollars.

Jericho4 for AI Distribution

Here’s another underappreciated aspect of Broadcom’s approach to the AI market. Case in point: The company is now shipping the Jericho4 Ethernet fabric router, which is a purpose-built platform for distributed AI infrastructure.

This router is specifically designed to interconnect over one million XPUs across multiple data centers. A single system scales to 36,000 HyperPorts, and each of these ports operates at a throughput of 3.2Tb per second. It also features deep buffering, line-rate MACsec encryption, and RoCE transport over distances exceeding 100 kilometers.

It’s like a massive brain. The GPUs or XPUs are the neurons that do the actual thinking, but without connections between them, we have just isolated neurons that can’t work together. In this case, Jericho4 will serve as the nervous system that connects all these neurons together.

In real-world cases, training the next generation of AI requires a cluster of 100,000 or more GPUs. These systems consume hundreds of megawatts of power, which is more electricity than most data centers can physically supply. So companies face a crossroads: either build a power plant for their data center (which is what Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) is doing) or find a way to spread their AI systems across multiple locations.

And since not everyone has as many resources as say, Microsoft, the choice is obvious. In this case, Jericho4 enables the second option, which means AI workloads can be distributed across multiple facilities while maintaining everything that’s needed for AI models to run.

The 3.2T HyperPort also combines four separate 800-gigabit Ethernet connections into a single logical port. With this setup, managing multiple connections wouldn’t be an issue. It’s also reported to boost utilization by 70%. So, if you’re trying to fill an Olympic-size swimming pool with water, you’d have one massive pipe that delivers sufficient water, not four smaller ones with different flow rates. This makes it easier to manage and more efficient, which is something that hyperscalers are aiming to achieve.

Although Nvidia certainly dominates AI training chips, they don’t have anything comparable to Jericho4 in networking. And no, NVLink is different; it’s for direct GPU-to-GPU communication, while Jericho4 is mainly for network switching infrastructure. That said, they might be used together in the same data center. Cisco and Arista Networks also have competing products, but I believe they lack Broadcom’s integration with customer AI silicon.

Personally, I think Broadcom has the experience in networking and custom silicon, and combining this with their relationships with hyperscalers, they now have a moat that’s practically impenetrable. Also, as AI workloads grow beyond what single data centers can handle (and there’s a high likelihood that they will), Broadcom will greatly benefit from this.

CEO Commitments

CEO Hock Tan has committed to maintaining leadership through 2030. Over the years, the CEO has led Broadcom's success through acquisition and organic growth. The man is 73 years old, but he extended his tenure to ensure continuity during this critical phase of AI expansion.

The board even approved a performance stock award linking the CEO’s compensation directly to AI revenue targets, with potential earnings of up to $616.6 million if the company achieves $120 billion in AI-driven revenue by 2030. This may have been overlooked by many, but this alignment between compensation and AI growth means the board is confident in the opportunities ahead of them.

Personally, I think having the CEO stay is worth at least 10% of the stock’s value, given his relationship with key customers and Broadcom’s track record under his leadership.

Regulatory Headwinds

However, there are challenges that Broadcom is facing, which include a regulatory storm in Europe.

For those who haven't followed the saga, CISPE (Cloud Infrastructure Services Providers in Europe) filed a legal complaint at the EU General Court citing concerns about the European Commission and its stance toward Broadcom following its VMware acquisition valued at $61 billion. I think this is a significant risk similar to others the larger companies face today.

The story allegedly goes like this. After Broadcom acquired VMware, they made changes to the business model, and according to the European Cloud Competition Observatory, Broadcom increased VMware licensing costs significantly, which affected many European customers. Specifically, the increase came from the removal of perpetual licenses (where you can buy once and own forever) in favor of bundled subscriptions, where you pay monthly or annually forever to retain ownership.

The outcry has been loud, and for good reason. I mean, speaking from personal experience, VMware’s virtualization tech is a foundation of many businesses. The software enabled companies to run multiple virtual computers on a single physical server, which made IT infrastructure far more efficient than a traditional setting. Also, VMware is said to control over 40% of the market, so it’s something that businesses can’t replace in a snap of a finger, which is why the regulators couldn’t wait to get involved.

That said, I think the risk is more manageable than it appears. The resolution will likely follow a predictable pattern based on similar cases. The European Commission already approved the VMware acquisition last summer (though it faces challenges), and they’ve already extracted some concessions. So, I think it's just a matter of time before a gentlemanly agreement is made.

AVGO Stock Performance

However, is Broadcom worth the premium? At the time of this publication, AVGO stock trades at $359.63 per share with a forward price-earnings (GAAP) of 84.45x, nearly triple that of the sector median of 31.73x. In terms of performance, the stock has delivered a 127% return over the past year and an astounding ~900% return over 5 years.

Given Broadcom’s growth runway, I’d say that the valuation is justified. While traditional semiconductor companies are challenged with cyclical demand and risks of technological obsolescence, Broadcom has built a solid foundation through its own custom silicon model. The result? Broadcom has created multi-year revenue streams that are supported by contracts worth tens of billions from hyperscalers.

And guess what? The first-mover advantage has positioned Broadcom to be part of these customers’ long-term roadmaps.

Personally, I think there’s still significant upside potential for AVGO, as most of us still don’t understand the company’s transformation. Wall Street analysts are slowly raising price targets, but I believe they’re still anchored to what they know: traditional semiconductor valuations. If AI revenue continues to grow in the following quarters, I expect valuations to re-rate higher.

Investment Verdict

Bringing all the pieces together, I think Broadcom is one of the most promising investment opportunities in the AI sector today. They’re already delivering growth and profitability with visibility into continued expansion. You’re also not betting on a startup that might run out of cash or selling some technology that might not work. Considering all the reasons above, I’m rating Broadcom a solid Strong Buy.

data.title: 8 Upcoming Dividend Increases
data.description:
Welcome back to another edition of upcoming dividend increases. I encourage investors to consider the power of dividend stocks, your pathway to steady and increasing income. There's nothing like sitting back and collecting regular paychecks from everyday companies simply by holding their shares. Adopting a dividend growth strategy encourages your returns to grow, making your financial future brighter. Now is an excellent time to invest in dividend stocks and enjoy the rewards they can bring.

Companies that regularly raise their dividends tend to be great long-term investments. Dividend increases suggest financial strength and stability, indicating that a company can generate reliable cash flow. It also requires deliberate capital allocation planning by management to make it a core tenet of the business. Consistent dividend growth typically indicates that a company is generating reliable cash flow, which in turn enables it to offer investors a combination of income and capital appreciation.

From the broader universe of investable stocks, I focus on those with a proven track record of annual dividend increases. I watch dividend increase announcements and combine those with other supporting data points. This information is core to my investment process, and I share it here with the Seeking Alpha community to help you make more informed investment decisions.

This week, I'll spotlight another group of companies and provide more detail on both the selections and the methodology behind them.

A few highlights from this week's list:

  • Average dividend increase: 7.1% (median: 5.1%)

  • Average dividend streak: 17 years (median: 12 years)

  • More regional banks comprise most of the list

  • Most stocks are near their highs at this time, except for JJSF, which may offer a compelling opportunity.

My strategy focuses on buying, holding, and adding to positions in companies that raise their dividends annually and have the potential to outperform relevant benchmarks over time.

How I Created The Lists

The information presented here is the result of combining multiple data sources: the "U.S. Dividend Champions" spreadsheet provides the universe of companies I review, and then upcoming dividend announcements. This combination joins data on companies with a track record of consistent dividend growth and the timeliness of their dividend increases. It's important to note that every company on this list has a minimum of 5 years of dividend growth history.

To be considered for this list, a company must offer higher total annual dividends. Although a company might not increase its dividend every calendar year, its overall annual dividend can still grow.

What Is The Ex-Dividend Date?

The ex-dividend date is when you must own shares to qualify for an upcoming dividend or distribution. To be eligible, you must have bought the shares by the end of the preceding business day. For instance, if the ex-dividend date is Tuesday, you must have acquired the shares by the market close on Monday. If the ex-dividend date falls on a Monday (or a Tuesday following a holiday on Monday), you must have purchased the shares by the previous Friday.

Dividend Streak Categories

Here are the definitions of the streak categories that I'll use throughout the piece.

  • King: 50+ years.
  • Champion/Aristocrat: 25+ years.
  • Contender: 10-24 years.
  • Challenger: 5+ years.
Category Count
King 0
Champion 1
Contender 6
Challenger 1

The Dividend Increases List

The data is sorted by the ex-dividend date (ascending) and then by the streak (descending):

Name Ticker Streak Forward Yield Ex-Div Date Increase Percent Streak Category
Community Trust Bancorp, Inc. (CTBI) 45 3.69 15-Sep-25 12.77% Champion
Stock Yards Bancorp, Inc. (SYBT) 16 1.66 15-Sep-25 3.23% Contender
Worthington Enterprises, Inc. Common Shares (WOR) 12 1.18 15-Sep-25 11.76% Contender
United Community Banks, Inc. (UCB) 11 3.07 15-Sep-25 4.17% Contender
Enterprise Financial Services Corporation (EFSC) 11 2.02 15-Sep-25 3.33% Contender
J & J Snack Foods Corp. (JJSF) 21 2.99 16-Sep-25 2.56% Contender
Avnet, Inc. (AVT) 12 2.6 17-Sep-25 6.06% Contender
Bank7 Corp.—Common stock (BSVN) 6 2.23 19-Sep-25 12.50% Challenger

Field Definitions

Streak: Years of dividend growth history are sourced from the U.S. Dividend Champions spreadsheet.

Forward Yield: The payout rate is calculated by dividing the new payout rate by the current share price.

Ex-Dividend Date: This is the date by which you must own the stock to receive the dividend.

Increase Percent: The percent increase.

Streak Category: This is the company's overall dividend history classification.

Show Me The Money

Here is a table that shows the new and old rates and the percentage increase. The table is sorted by ex-dividend day in ascending order and dividend streak in descending order.

Ticker Old Rate New Rate Increase Percent
CTBI 0.47 0.53 12.77%
SYBT 0.31 0.32 3.23%
WOR 0.17 0.19 11.76%
UCB 0.24 0.25 4.17%
EFSC 0.3 0.31 3.33%
JJSF 0.78 0.8 2.56%
AVT 0.33 0.35 6.06%
BSVN 0.24 0.27 12.50%

Additional Metrics

Here are additional metrics related to these companies. Some data points include yearly pricing action and the P/E ratio. The table is sorted the same way as the table above.

Ticker Current Price 52-Week Low 52-Week High PE Ratio % Off Low % Off High
CTBI 57.46 44.6 61.67 10.54 29% Off Low 7% Off High
SYBT 77.17 56.35 83.83 16.19 37% Off Low 8% Off High
WOR 64.63 37.88 70.91 4.18 71% Off Low 9% Off High
EFSC 61.32 45.22 63.13 12.56 36% Off Low 3% Off High
UCB 32.53 22.93 35.38 12.27 42% Off Low 8% Off High
JJSF 106.89 106.4 180.8 153.67 0% Off Low 41% Off High
AVT 53.93 39.22 59.24 11.80 38% Off Low 9% Off High
BSVN 48.51 32.49 50.26 5.97 49% Off Low 3% Off High

Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the "Chowder Rule," the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
CTBI 3.69 27.7 13.7 9.1 7 12.7
UCB 3.07 4.4 5.4 6.2 18.2 9.2
JJSF 2.99 6.1 7.2 7 8.4 9.9
AVT 2.6 6.5 9.7 9.5 7.5 12.1
BSVN 2.23 14.3 26.9 19.1 21.3
EFSC 2.02 12.9 11.6 10.6 18 12.6
SYBT 1.66 3.3 3.5 3 7.3 4.6
WOR 1.18 -29.2 -15.3 -6.7 -0.6 -5.5

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market and grow their dividend payouts over time. For broad exposure to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector, I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

SCHD features strong historical performance, offers a yield that exceeds that of the S&P 500, and consistently delivers increasing dividends. With over $69B in assets, it's an incredibly popular dividend-growth ETF. The ten-year dividend growth rate is one of the four key factors that SCHD tracks in its index.

Based on this analysis (and further due diligence), I have included several companies in my portfolio and will sometimes make timely purchases of existing holdings. The charts below assume dividends are reinvested.

For reference, SCHD returned about 218%, or 12% per year. We have a couple of hot dogs in this group with Worthington Enterprises and Stock Yards Bancorp. WOR rocked it with a 432% return, and SYBT with a 317% return.

Then we have SCHD in third place and EFSC in a close fourth, with a 201% total return.

Finally, we start to bring up the back side of the pack. CTBI (138%), UCB (109%), AVT (57%), and finally JJSF (10%).

This is where I would say to a value investor, JJSF may be interesting here. Imagine the company has essentially returned nothing over a decade and is 40%+ off its 52-week high. I think this could be worth a look if you like turnaround stories.

Next Steps

I'm selective about the individual holdings in my portfolio. In my individual sleeve portion of my portfolio, I have 33 holdings. The bar is quite high due to the diminishing returns of adding an additional holding. I'm looking for companies that offer significant alpha over SCHD and stand out from it. Furthermore, I believe in the importance of consistent, strong earnings and dividend growth over time. These articles provide me (and I hope you) a chance to review what's performed well and to take a closer look at my current holdings.

I'm actually interested in taking a look at JJSF as a turnaround idea and learning more about why they've returned nothing over the past decade.

I'd love to hear your thoughts on my strategy, so feel free to share yours in the comments below! As always, do your due diligence before making any investment decisions.

data.title: Deere & Company: Still Find The Valuation Too Rich For My Liking
data.description:
Investment Action

I had a hold rating for Deere & Company (NYSE:DE) previously, as I thought the valuation did not provide a reasonable margin of safety. While DE has shown impressive operational improvements and may be establishing a structurally higher earnings floor, the near-term cyclical headwinds are worsening. Weak equipment demand, tariff pressures, and pricing weakness in construction & forestry leave little room for upside surprises. Against this backdrop, the valuation still looks stretched relative to history, and as such, I reiterate my hold rating.

A Structurally Stronger Company (Potentially)

A very encouraging development so far is that DE’s earnings may have found a floor at a structurally higher level. I emphasize "may" because it has not come true yet. This potential floor is based on management’s FY25 outlook that calls for operating margins of 15.5–17% in Production & Precision Ag and 12–13.5% in Small Ag & Turf. For those who have not followed DE over the years, holding margins in the mid-teens range through a significant downturn is not just impressive, but it suggests that DE’s profitability baseline has shifted upward. I would think this lift is due to a richer product mix, a growing stream of recurring revenue from Precision Ag, and better cost discipline. If this is true (DE really achieved this guide), I think this limits downside risk in the near term and sets a stronger base for recovery when demand turns.

There are tangible progresses that give confidence regarding this guide. For instance, DE’s progress on inventory management is solid proof of cost discipline. To be specific, DE has aggressively right-sized dealer channels and cleared out excess equipment across its key markets. In North America, high-horsepower tractor inventories are down 45% y/y, with combines down 25%. Brazil, which I called out previously as an important growth driver, has seen even better progress, with large equipment inventories more than halved from late 2023 peaks. This is a very meaningful achievement because by aligning dealer stock with end-user demand, DE has removed a major overhang heading into FY26. It has effectively reduced the risk of deep discounting and ensures that factory output can be matched more closely with retail sales. In other words, it is in a solid position to protect margins.

Another thing that adds confidence is DE’s ability to command price in a weak demand environment. DE has announced list price increases for model year 2026 equipment: 4–4.5% for sprayers and ~3% for large tractors. We have to see how volume plays out, but suppose demand levels are unaffected; it would really suggest DE has a strong brand and that clients are willing to pay more for the technology (again, this protects margins).

Severe Near-Term Headwinds Cloud the Near-Term Outlook

While the improvements and potentially higher earnings floor are encouraging for the long term, I think investors completely ignore the near-term cyclical headwinds that are impacting the business today. The core concern I highlighted in my previous write-up, weak equipment demand, has gotten worse, with new data confirming that the agricultural equipment market is heading into a third consecutive year of demand contraction. The most solid evidence comes from DE’s FY26 Early Order Programs, which provide a forward look into farmer purchasing intentions. And it is not pretty. Management revealed that orders for sprayers are down a steep 20% y/y. Commentary on planters and combines was also bearish, suggesting broad-based weakness across key product lines in North America. The way management had framed this cycle is also concerning. Specifically, they noted that while tractors and combines are in the second year of a downturn, sprayers are only in year one, implying that certain parts of the portfolio may have further room to fall before finding a bottom. Viewed together, what all these data points are saying is that a near-term recovery in equipment revenue is highly unlikely, and I expect this to continue to weigh on investor sentiment, as the market will unlikely look past the negative headline demand trends.

The pressure is not limited to the agricultural segment. The Construction & Forestry segment is also facing competitive pressures (management explicitly used the word “heightened”), particularly in the North American earthmoving equipment market. Unlike the agricultural segment, where DE can exercise pricing power, this is not the case here. This competition pressure has forced DE to implement additional incentive programs to move inventory, resulting in negative price realization in 3Q25. Consequently, DE was forced to lower its FY25 pricing outlook for this segment, which is now expected to be down ~2% for the year, a revision from the prior forecast of down 1%. This suggests that DE is effectively forced to sacrifice price to defend market share, which is a very negative dynamic that could further pressure margins and make an earnings recovery more challenging in the near term.

There are also cost pressures to consider. The impact from tariffs has proven to be more severe than I expected—management recently increased its forecast for FY25 pre-tax impact of tariffs by $100 million, from $500 million to $600 million. This revision was driven by higher reciprocal rates from Europe and an increase in steel and aluminum tariffs from 25% to 50%. This $100 million is a direct hit to profitability that will create a drag on margins, which, unfortunately, offsets the upside made through pricing and cost controls.

Valuation

As expected, DE’s valuation was too rich previously—now downgraded to 22.6x NTM PE (~2x downgrade from June’s level). I still think the valuation is too high here. For a business that is still facing visible near-term cyclical weakness, I don’t see a reason why it should trade at a premium to its historical average of 17x. Some premium may make sense due to the better earnings profile (more recurring revenue), but the current level is too much. As DE prints more weak results ahead, I see more downside than upside, as the market may revise estimates downwards to better reflect the cyclical weakness.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for DE. The long-term story around DE’s recurring revenue and improved cost discipline remains very much intact, but the business is still navigating the toughest part of the cycle. With orders falling, tariffs rising, and margins pressured, I believe the risk/reward balance remains skewed to the downside at current multiples. Until there is clearer evidence that demand has bottomed and earnings visibility improves, I think the wise move is to stay on the sidelines.

data.title: Synopsys: Load Up While The Market Ignores
data.description:
Synopsys: A Gift Or Not?

A gift or not a gift? Investors who saw the stunning decline in the stock of Synopsys, Inc. (NASDAQ:SNPS) are arguably trying to assess whether it is picking up the falling knife. Following an initial post-earnings crash that saw SNPS give up more than 30%, dip buyers appeared ready to defend it against a further slide, as SNPS regained almost 13% to finish Thursday's trading.

As a software company focusing on the foundry customers within the semiconductor value chain, investing in SNPS was always going to be mired in concentration risks, isn't it? Think about it. How many leading-edge foundries do we have in the market right now? I'm sure you can easily name three: TSMC (TSM), Intel (INTC), and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF).

Hence, a wrong bet could also have substantive ramifications on the backlog and growth prospects. In the case of Synopsys, while management didn't specify the exact customer's name, it's not rocket science for us to make an educated guess that Intel's fumbles with its 18A process roadmap has had a significant impact on Synopsys's IP business. And if we add on the geopolitical headwinds emanating from SNPS's Chinese exposure, it seems like a double whammy of sorts affecting it all at the same time. Even if it doesn't quite qualify as a perfect storm, calling it a maelstrom wouldn't have surprised investors.

Synopsys: Don't Be Mistaken About Its Core Drivers

Yet, I don't think any SNPS investor is mistaken about the core growth drivers underlining its dominance and competitive moat. It's clear that EDA will remain as its most significant growth vector through the rest of the decade. Management promulgated that the tailwinds from increasingly complex high-performance computing and AI workloads will be key underpinnings for SNPS's design automation business.

And when the company manages to fully integrate the synergies and capabilities originating from Ansys, it should strengthen its offerings, while driving demand for Synopsys' emulation and prototyping solutions. In addition, SNPS has also been focusing on using AI-powered EDA, and has secured 20 customers in the pilot phase, opening up another potentially beneficial growth pillar to its design business.

Also, I think the market might have also underplayed possibilities from the transition into more customized subsystems, which will most certainly increase the deal size and royalties opportunities that should benefit its IP business, that is currently under siege.

SNPS: EDA Remains The Key Pillar

Hence, I'm perplexed why the market decided to eviscerate the YTD gains accrued painstakingly by SNPS investors due to near-term weaknesses imputed to its IP business. Notably, the IP business suffered an 8% decline due to the challenges highlighted earlier, even though the EDA business notched a 23% YoY rise, including revenue from Ansys. And with EDA still accounting for more than 70% of the company's overall trailing twelve months revenue, I believe the negative reaction from the market appears to be too dramatic than what the numbers suggest.

Nevertheless, I can comprehend the disappointment, as execution was clearly amiss with its FQ3 miss, coupled with lower-than-expected guidance, further hampering sentiments. While I think the market has the right to air its grievances (making it loud and clear), it seems to have openly ignored the long-term confidence from management in its ability to recover its IP business, while also riding on the structural tailwinds that should bolster its EDA business.

As a reminder, management hasn't wavered from its commitment to deliver a mid-40% operating margin outlook, which I thought should have allayed fears of a structural decline. Wall Street's estimates suggest an adjusted operating margin of 39.6% for FY2026, indicating that while near-term profitability headwinds in FY2025 could hamper its business, we shouldn't expect integration risks to materially affect its margin trajectory.

Buy Or Sell SNPS Stock?

And I guess the market seems to agree with that proposition based on the almost 13% recovery yesterday. If you glean the chart above, we can clearly observe robust buying support that has benefited SNPS over the past two years whenever the stock falls toward its 10Y average of 21.7x. At that level, buyers had consistently returned with a fervor to defend it against a further slide.

And with the stock still hovering close to that level due to its recent plunge, I believe investors should seriously consider capitalizing on a solid opportunity to buy into a high-quality software play with a highly robust and profitable business model.

While I'm not about to ignore the China-related risks that could still inflict unanticipated damage on buying sentiments, or the uncertainties relating to Intel's business, I believe these headwinds seem to have been de-risked sufficiently for us to take a more bullish posture now.

Rating: Buy.

data.title: Constellation Software: An AI Winner In The Shadows
data.description:
Constellation Software (TSX:CSU:CA) is one of the most special companies in the world. I would say it is the best serial acquirer in existence. It is commanded by one of the best and strangest CEOs on the planet, who has 6 executing arms with the power to make decentralized M&A decisions.

There are two narratives that have been prevailing in the market for some time now:

  • Constellation has run out of acquisition TAMs in the Vertical Software (VMS) segment.
  • AI will be disruptive to their business.

In this article my aim is to question these two points.

Moreover, my current interest in the company arises because it is a company that I have known well for years (I have been holding it since 2021 at $1600 in a portfolio I have with my father, but not in my personal one) and because it has never fallen more than 30% in its history. Well, recently, it dropped almost -20% from all-time highs.

All values are in CAD despite the $ symbol.

Inorganic Growth

It all starts at Constellations's annual meeting in May of this year.

It is a fact that CSU is reaching a considerable size.

Despite having a TAM of acquisitions in the Vertical Software niches in which the company is fully specialized (40,000-80,000 possible companies), many are small in size, which does not “move the needle” when they buy them, and they have to buy an increasing number of companies, as is shown in the second row.

What is starting to be raised in the company is:

  • Either larger purchases with lower returns (closer to 15%-20% vs. the current 25%-30%).
  • Expansion into other sectors that are not related to the company's traditional expertise but still offer returns of +25%.

And at the annual meeting, it was strongly implied that they are seriously considering this. They say they have full confidence in CSU's decentralized structure and will rely on what the heads of the divisions will buy.

Constellation Software operates as a decentralized holding company where its 6 parent companies (Vertical Market Software groups) operate autonomously, buying and managing niche software companies with minimal central intervention. These 6 groups are: Volaris Group, Harris Computer Systems, Jonas Software, Perseus Group, Trapeze Group, and Vela Software.

So it is quite possible that over time we will see CSU enter new market niches, which we probably can't even think of right now.

An example of this could be payments, as they said at their annual meeting:

We're definitely focused on payments, and we've made a couple of acquisitions in that space.

We're looking at others, and I think it's something that can bring tremendous value.

And what we see from our competitors in this space is a lot of payments and software coupling.

So if we want to remain competitive and leaders in our field, we have to be better on the payments side.

So we'll continue to make inroads in that space and, hopefully, we'll get better quarter over quarter.

- Barry Symons, CEO, Annual General Meeting 2025

Organic Growth

CSU's organic growth has always been an issue, hence the spin-offs of Topicus and Lumin, which did have more organic growth and could benefit from their expertise to drive this part of the growth equation.

Anyway, something seems to have changed for the better in this industry after the rise of AI.

They commented that AI can help vertical software companies a lot because it will save a lot of time and effort when it comes to chopping code, making it economically viable, being able to add additional services to those VMS at a reduced price, and unlocking pricing power never unlocked before. This could solve one of the biggest fears that has always surrounded the matrix.

This started to show in the Q2 results.

Constellation has achieved its highest organic growth since 2023. The stock was up 4.4% on the day of the presentation, which, for a company like CSU, is a significant move.

Otherwise:

Revenue: +15% to $2.84B [Organic growth: +5%]

-Net revenue: -68% to $56M

-CFO: +63% to $433M

-FCF to shareholders: +20% to $220M

-Acquisitions: $380M aggregate cash consideration ($469M incl. deferred payments)

Very important growth in Maintenance & Other Recurring (+7% organic, the fastest in the group), which, as its name indicates, is the most recurring part of the company.

Right now the organics at Constellation are quite important, or more than they were before, because ex-organic M&A would be 10%, which is fine, but far from what it was.... In fact, they are not managing to deploy 100% of what they generate, although it is also true that they have not made any significant large acquisitions.

One month after these results, my feelings that the AI will eventually benefit Constellation are becoming more and more latent every day. It will be possible to obtain double-digit EBIT growth in the mid-single digits, with low organic sales growth (e.g., 2%).

The folks at In Practise published a piece of an interview with a Topicus executive, in which they commented as follows:
